turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court in case 16-1011, WesternGeco v. ION Geophysical.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on the Writ of Certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. Petitioner WesternGeco owns patents for a system that is used to survey the ocean floor. Respondent ION Geophysical began selling a competing system by manufacturing components in the United States, shipping them abroad and assembling them into a system indistinguishable from that of WesternGeco's. WesternGeco sued for patent infringement under Section 271(f) of the Patent Act. The jury found ION liable and awarded WesternGeco damages for lost profit under Section 284 of the Patent Act. ION moved to set aside the verdict arguing that WesternGeco could not recover lost profits because the relevant provisions of the Patent Act do not apply extraterritorially. The District Court denied the motion, but the Federal Circuit reversed. It reasoned that Section 271(f)(2) does not allow patent owners to recover damages for lost foreign profits. In an opinion filed with the clerk today we reverse. We hold that whether or not Section 284 of the Patent Act applies extraterritorially, WesternGeco's award for lost profits was a domestic application of the statute. The presumption against extraterritoriality assumes that federal statutes apply only within the United States. To determine whether a statute is being applied extraterritorially courts identify the statute's focus and then ask whether the conduct relevant to that focus occurred in the United States. If the focus occurred in the United States, then the case involves permissible domestic application of the statute. Here the focus of the relevant provisions of the patent is on the act of patent infringement. This Court's precedents explain that the overriding purpose of Section 284 is to afford patent owners complete compensation for infringements. In cases involving Section 271(f)(2), the infringement is the act of supplying components from the United States. That conduct is domestic. Thus, the lost profits award in this case was a domestic application of Section 284 and extraterritoriality concerns are not present. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Federal Circuit. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion, which Justice Breyer has joined.
